topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The vase sat on the kitchen counter, a garish splash of pink and yellow against the otherwise sterile white granite. I picked up a stem, the silk cool beneath my fingertips, and immediately felt a tightening in my chest, a familiar knot forming just beneath my ribs. Who sends these? It’s never anyone who actually cares. I stared at the arrangement—a clumsy mix of lilies and something aggressively cheerful—and traced the edges of the paper wrapping. There was nothing inside, naturally. No note, no explanation, just the silent, unsolicited offering. I dropped the flowers back down, the clatter of the ceramic sounding loud in the quiet apartment. I didn't even bother to look at the delivery notification; I just wanted it gone, a misplaced object demanding attention.

[story 2]

My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles strain in my neck. The bouquet was sitting on the entryway table, an unwelcome intrusion into the neat order I’d painstakingly established. I circled it slowly, my gaze sharp, cataloging the cheap plastic wrapping and the overly bright hues. Every glance felt like an accusation. I wanted to snatch them up, to crumple the paper into a tight ball and throw it into the bin, but some stubborn inertia kept my hand hovering above them. It was a performance, I thought bitterly. A gesture meant to smooth over some unspoken obligation, another thing I wasn't prepared to perform. I finally snatched them, the petals feeling flimsy and utterly meaningless in my grip, and marched them straight to the balcony, where the evening air felt cold and indifferent.

[story 3]

I watched the delivery driver leave, the sound of the engine fading into the city hum. The flowers were left on the doorstep, abandoned. They looked so hopeful, so utterly devoid of context. I walked over, my steps deliberate and heavy. I didn't touch them immediately. Instead, I leaned in close, inhaling the cloying sweetness, and a wave of sourness washed over me. It felt like being handed a burden, something saccharine and pointless that I now had to pretend to acknowledge. I turned away abruptly, my shoulders hunching forward as if bracing for impact. I needed to get rid of this evidence of someone else's misplaced sentimentality before it started to settle in my own space.
